title: Linux Essentials
summary: Master the Linux command line: filesystem, permissions, processes, pipes, and SSH. The foundation every engineer needs.
guide:
# Linux Essentials

## What Even Is Linux? (Start Here If You Are New)

Imagine your computer is a restaurant. The **hardware** (CPU, RAM, disk) is the kitchen equipment. The **operating system** is the head chef who controls everything — decides who gets to use the grills, hands out ingredients, and keeps order. Linux is one such head chef, and it runs on almost every server, cloud machine, Docker container, and CI/CD pipeline in the world.

You have likely used Windows or macOS as your personal computer OS. Linux is different — it was designed from the ground up for servers, automation, and multi-user environments. It is fast, stable, free, and open source (anyone can read or modify its code).

**Why should a developer learn Linux?**
- Your code runs on Linux servers (AWS EC2, GCP, Azure — all Linux under the hood)
- Docker containers ARE Linux
- CI/CD pipelines run bash scripts on Linux runners
- SSH into any production machine = you are in a Linux terminal
- Kubernetes pods run Linux containers

```
Your laptop (macOS/Windows)
        |
        | SSH connection
        v
Linux Server (AWS EC2, GCP VM, VPS...)
  - Ubuntu 22.04 LTS  (most popular for servers)
  - CentOS / RHEL     (enterprise)
  - Alpine Linux      (used inside Docker - tiny 5MB)
```

---

## The Terminal - Your New Best Friend

When you work with Linux, you do not click around a graphical UI. You type commands in a **terminal** (also called shell, command line, or CLI - Command Line Interface).

**Analogy:** Think of the terminal like texting your computer. Instead of clicking 'New Folder', you type `mkdir my-folder`. It feels weird at first, but it is 10x faster once you know the language.

The most common shell is **bash** (Bourne Again Shell). When you open a terminal, you see a prompt:

```
alice@myserver:~$
  |       |    | |
  |       |    | +-- $ means regular user  (# means root/admin)
  |       |    +---- ~ means you are in your home directory
  |       +--------- hostname (the server name)
  +----------------- your username
```

You type a command, press Enter, see the output, done:

```bash
alice@myserver:~$ echo "Hello Linux"
Hello Linux
alice@myserver:~$        <-- prompt returns, ready for next command
```

---

## The Filesystem - Everything Is a File

Linux has one giant directory tree. Everything lives inside it — programs, configs, logs, even hardware devices. This is different from Windows which has C:\ D:\ etc.

```
/                          <- the root - the very top of everything
|
+-- bin/                   <- essential programs: ls, cp, mv, echo
+-- etc/                   <- ALL configuration files live here
|   +-- nginx/
|   |   +-- nginx.conf     <- Nginx web server config
|   +-- ssh/
|   |   +-- sshd_config    <- SSH server settings
|   +-- hosts              <- hostname to IP mappings
+-- home/                  <- user home directories
|   +-- alice/             <- alice personal space (written as ~)
|   +-- bob/
+-- var/                   <- data that changes over time
|   +-- log/               <- LOG FILES live here!
|       +-- syslog         <- system-wide events
|       +-- auth.log       <- login attempts
|       +-- nginx/access.log  <- every HTTP request
+-- tmp/                   <- temporary files - WIPED on reboot
+-- usr/                   <- user-installed programs
|   +-- bin/               <- programs installed via apt
+-- proc/                  <- VIRTUAL: shows running processes as files
+-- dev/                   <- device files (disks, terminals)
    +-- sda                <- your primary hard drive
```

**Memory trick - what each folder means:**
- `/etc` = The HR / Policy office (all the rules and configs)
- `/var/log` = The security camera room (audit logs)
- `/home/alice` = Alice's personal office
- `/tmp` = A whiteboard erased every morning
- `/usr/bin` = Where all the installed software tools live
- `/proc` = A live snapshot of what the system is doing RIGHT NOW

**Real-world scenario:** Your Spring Boot app crashes at 3am. You SSH in and:
1. Go to `/var/log/` to find your app log file
2. Run `tail -100 /var/log/myapp/app.log` to see the last 100 lines
3. Spot the `OutOfMemoryError`, understand it, fix the heap setting

---

## Navigating the Filesystem - Moving Around

These are the commands you will type hundreds of times per day:

```bash
# WHERE AM I RIGHT NOW?
pwd
# Output: /home/alice
# pwd = Print Working Directory

# WHAT IS IN THIS FOLDER?
ls              # basic list
ls -l           # long format: shows permissions, size, date
ls -a           # show hidden files too (files starting with .)
ls -lah         # everything: long + all + human-readable sizes
# Example output:
# drwxr-xr-x  3 alice alice 4.0K Jan 15 09:00 projects/
# -rw-r--r--  1 alice alice  82K Jan 14 18:30 notes.txt
# d = directory, - = regular file

# MOVE TO A DIFFERENT FOLDER
cd /etc                # go to /etc  (absolute path - starts from /)
cd projects            # go to projects/ folder (relative - from where you are)
cd ..                  # go up one level (like clicking back)
cd ~                   # go to YOUR home directory
cd -                   # go back to where you just were

# CREATE FOLDERS AND FILES
mkdir photos           # make a new folder
mkdir -p a/b/c/d       # make nested folders all at once
touch notes.txt        # create empty file

# COPY, MOVE, RENAME, DELETE
cp file.txt backup.txt        # copy a file
cp -r folder/ backup_folder/  # copy a folder (need -r for recursive)
mv old.txt new.txt            # rename a file
mv file.txt /tmp/             # move file to /tmp folder
rm file.txt                   # delete a file (NO RECYCLE BIN!)
rm -rf folder/                # delete folder and EVERYTHING inside
```

> WARNING: `rm -rf` has no undo. There is no trash. Always double-check before running it.

---

## Reading Files - Viewing Content

```bash
cat file.txt          # print the whole file on screen
less file.txt         # page through (q to quit, arrow keys to scroll, / to search)
head -20 file.txt     # show first 20 lines only
tail -20 file.txt     # show last 20 lines only
tail -f app.log       # LIVE VIEW - shows new lines as they appear
                      # perfect for watching logs in real time! Ctrl+C to stop

# SEARCH INSIDE FILES
grep "ERROR" app.log               # find lines containing ERROR
grep -i "error" app.log            # same but case-insensitive
grep -r "database" /etc/           # search inside ALL files in /etc
grep -n "NullPointer" app.log      # show line numbers with matches
grep -A 3 -B 3 "FATAL" app.log     # show 3 lines before and after each match
grep -c "ERROR" app.log            # just count how many lines match
```

**Real-world scenario:** App is throwing errors in production. You run:
```bash
tail -f /var/log/myapp/app.log | grep ERROR
```
This pipes tail into grep - you see ONLY error lines as they appear, live. You can spot patterns instantly.

---

## File Permissions - Who Can Do What

Every single file in Linux has **access rules** for three groups of people. This matters a LOT in production - wrong permissions = app crashes or security holes.

```
-rw-r--r--  1  alice  developers  4096  Jan 15  report.txt
|          |   |      |
|          |   |      +- group name (developers)
|          |   +-------- owner name (alice)
|          +------------ number of hard links
+----------------------- permission string (10 characters)
```

**The 10-character permission string explained:**
```
  -  r w -  r - -  r - -
  |  |   |  |   |  |   |
  |  +---+  +---+  +---+
  |  owner  group  others
  |
  + file type: - = regular file
               d = directory
               l = symbolic link (shortcut)
```

**What r, w, x mean:**
```
r = read    = can you open and read this file?       (value = 4)
w = write   = can you change or delete this file?   (value = 2)
x = execute = can you run this as a program?        (value = 1)
- = no permission for this slot
```

**How the numbers work in chmod:**
```
You add up the values to get one number per group:

rwx = 4+2+1 = 7   (full access)
rw- = 4+2+0 = 6   (read + write, no execute)
r-x = 4+0+1 = 5   (read + execute, no write)
r-- = 4+0+0 = 4   (read only)
--- = 0+0+0 = 0   (no access whatsoever)

chmod 755 script.sh
       |||
       ||+-- others: 5 = r-x (can read and run, cannot write)
       |+--- group:  5 = r-x
       +---- owner:  7 = rwx (full access)

chmod 600 private_key
       |||
       ||+-- others: 0 = --- (NO access at all)
       |+--- group:  0 = --- (NO access at all)
       +---- owner:  6 = rw- (can read and write)
```

**Common permission recipes to memorize:**
```bash
chmod 755 script.sh        # shell scripts: owner full, everyone can read+run
chmod 644 config.txt       # config files: owner can edit, everyone can read
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa    # SSH private key: ONLY owner reads (SSH refuses if wrong!)
chmod 700 ~/.ssh/          # .ssh folder: only owner can enter
chmod +x deploy.sh         # add execute for everyone (keeps other permissions)
chmod -R 755 /var/www/     # -R = recursive: apply to all files inside
```

**chown - change who owns a file:**
```bash
chown alice report.txt              # change owner to alice
chown alice:developers report.txt   # change owner AND group
sudo chown -R www-data /var/www/html  # recursive ownership change
```

**Real-world pitfall:** Your app cannot write to its log folder:
```bash
ls -la /var/log/
# drwxr-xr-x  2  root  root  4096  Jan 15  myapp/
# Problem: folder is owned by root! Your app user cannot write to it.
# Fix:
sudo chown -R myappuser:myappuser /var/log/myapp/
```

---

## Processes - Running Programs

When you run a program, the OS creates a **process** - an instance of the program with its own chunk of memory and a unique **PID** (Process ID - just a number).

```
You type: java -jar app.jar
                |
                v
Kernel creates a process:
  PID:    4521
  Memory: 512MB heap allocated
  Files:  opens app.jar, log file, network sockets
  CPU:    scheduled to run on available core
                |
                v
Process runs until:
  - Normal exit   (code calls System.exit(0))
  - Error crash   (unhandled exception)
  - You kill it   (SIGTERM or SIGKILL)
```

**Viewing processes:**
```bash
ps aux
# Shows ALL running processes
# a=all users  u=user-friendly format  x=include background
# Output:
# USER   PID  %CPU  %MEM  COMMAND
# alice  4521  25.3  12.8  java -jar app.jar
# root    234   0.1   0.2  nginx: master process

top           # live updating process viewer (like Windows Task Manager)
htop          # prettier version of top (install: sudo apt install htop)
              # q to quit

pgrep nginx   # find the PID of process named nginx
pgrep -a java # find all java processes with their full command
```

**Stopping processes:**
```bash
kill 4521       # send SIGTERM - politely ask the process to stop
kill -9 4521    # send SIGKILL - immediate force kill (no cleanup)
killall java    # kill ALL processes named java

# ALWAYS try SIGTERM first!
# SIGTERM = like asking a chef to finish the dish before going home
# SIGKILL = like cutting the power - no chance to save anything
```

**Running in background:**
```bash
./long_script.sh &    # & = run in background, get prompt back immediately
nohup ./server.sh &   # nohup = keep running even after terminal closes
jobs                  # list background jobs in this terminal
fg %1                 # bring job 1 to foreground
Ctrl+Z                # pause/suspend current program
Ctrl+C                # stop/kill current program
```

---

## Pipes and Redirection - The Unix Superpower

This is where Linux becomes incredibly powerful. You can **chain commands together** — the output of one command becomes the input of the next. It is like an assembly line.

```
command1  |  command2  |  command3
    |            |            |
    |      takes command1     |
    |      output as input    +-- takes command2 output as input
    +-- runs first, produces output
```

**Examples:**
```bash
# Count ERROR lines in a log file
grep 'ERROR' app.log | wc -l

# Find top 10 IPs making POST requests
cat access.log | grep 'POST' | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
#     |               |               |            |        |           |          |
#  read file      keep POSTs    get 1st column  sort   count     sort by count  top 10

# Find all java processes and show only their PIDs
ps aux | grep java | awk '{print $2}'
```

**Redirection - sending output to files instead of screen:**
```bash
ls -la > filelist.txt        # > OVERWRITES the file (creates if not exists)
ls -la >> filelist.txt       # >> APPENDS to end of file
cat nope.txt 2> errors.txt   # 2> redirects only ERROR output
java -jar app.jar > app.log 2>&1  # both normal AND error output to one file
                                   # 2>&1 means 'send stderr to wherever stdout goes'

# Useful pattern: run app in background, log everything:
nohup java -jar app.jar > app.log 2>&1 &
```

---

## SSH - Connecting to Remote Servers

SSH (Secure Shell) lets you control a remote Linux server as if you are sitting at it. All traffic is encrypted - nobody can snoop on your commands.

```
Your Laptop                      Remote Server
    |                                 |
    |  === encrypted SSH tunnel ===>  |
    |                           you control it
    |                           run commands
    |                           read files
    |                           restart services
```

**Connecting to a server:**
```bash
ssh alice@192.168.1.50           # connect by IP address
ssh alice@myserver.com           # connect by hostname
ssh -p 2222 alice@server.com     # use port 2222 (default is 22)
ssh -i ~/.ssh/mykey.pem ec2-user@3.14.159.26  # AWS EC2 with key file
```

**SSH Key Authentication (better than passwords):**
```bash
# STEP 1: Generate a key pair on YOUR OWN machine
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "my work laptop key"
# This creates TWO files:
#   ~/.ssh/id_ed25519      <- PRIVATE KEY (never share this, ever!)
#   ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub  <- PUBLIC KEY  (safe to give to servers)

# STEP 2: Put your public key on the server
ssh-copy-id alice@192.168.1.50
# Or manually append to server's ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

# STEP 3: Connect without password!
ssh alice@192.168.1.50
```

**How it works (simplified):**
```
Your public key  ---> goes to the server  (like a padlock)
Your private key ---> stays on your laptop (like the key)

Server says: "Prove you have the key for this padlock"
Your laptop signs a challenge with private key, sends the signature
Server checks the signature with public key
Server: "Valid! Come in."
You never send the private key over the network!
```

**Copying files over SSH:**
```bash
scp report.txt alice@server:/home/alice/      # copy TO server
scp alice@server:/var/log/app.log ./          # copy FROM server
scp -r ./project alice@server:/home/alice/   # copy entire folder
```

**Common SSH problem - Permission denied (publickey):**
```
Fix checklist:
1. chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519       (private key must be owner-only)
2. chmod 700 ~/.ssh/                 (.ssh folder must be owner-only)
3. Check public key is in server ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
4. Use correct username (ec2-user for AWS, ubuntu for Ubuntu AMIs)
5. Verify server IP/hostname is correct
6. Check SSH service: sudo systemctl status ssh
```

---

## Package Management - Installing Software

In Linux you do not download installers from websites. You use a **package manager** - a built-in tool that downloads, installs, and updates software safely.

**Ubuntu/Debian (apt):**
```bash
sudo apt update              # first: refresh what packages are available
sudo apt install nginx       # install nginx web server
sudo apt install -y openjdk-17-jdk  # -y = yes to all prompts
sudo apt remove nginx        # uninstall
sudo apt upgrade             # upgrade all installed packages
apt search "text editor"    # search for packages
apt show nginx               # get details about a package
```

**CentOS / RHEL / Fedora (dnf):**
```bash
sudo dnf update
sudo dnf install nginx
sudo dnf remove nginx
```

**What is sudo?**
Most system commands need **root** (admin) privileges. `sudo` runs that ONE command as root, then drops back:
```bash
sudo apt install nginx    # runs apt install as root (asks your password)
sudo systemctl restart nginx
sudo nano /etc/nginx/nginx.conf  # edit a protected config file
```

---

## Environment Variables - Configuring Programs

Environment variables are **name=value** settings that programs can read. This is how you pass database URLs, API keys, and config to your app without hardcoding.

```bash
env                    # see ALL environment variables
echo $HOME             # /home/alice
echo $USER             # alice
echo $PATH             # /usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin

# Set for current session only
export DB_URL="postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb"

# Set permanently (survives terminal restarts)
echo 'export DB_URL="postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc       # reload - makes it active NOW
```

**How PATH works:**
```
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
          |              |      |
          |              |      +-- check /bin/ for the command
          |              +--------- check /usr/bin/ next
          +------------------------ check /usr/local/bin/ FIRST

When you type: java
Linux searches each PATH folder left-to-right until it finds 'java'
If not found: 'command not found' error

Fix: if java is at /opt/java/bin/java, add to PATH:
export PATH=$PATH:/opt/java/bin
```

---

## System Health - Monitoring Your Server

```bash
# DISK SPACE
df -h         # disk free - shows all filesystems with human-readable sizes
# Output:
# Filesystem   Size   Used  Avail  Use%  Mounted on
# /dev/sda1     50G    23G    25G   48%  /
du -sh /var/log/   # disk usage of a specific folder
du -sh /*          # disk usage of all top-level folders

# RAM
free -h       # show memory usage
# Output:
#             total   used   free   available
# Mem:         15Gi   8Gi    2Gi    6Gi

# UPTIME AND LOAD
uptime        # how long system has been running + load averages

# HARDWARE INFO
lscpu         # CPU info (cores, speed, architecture)
ip addr       # network interfaces and IP addresses
cat /etc/os-release   # what distro and version is this?
uname -a      # kernel version
```

---

## Editing Text Files

```bash
# NANO - beginner friendly editor
nano /etc/hosts
# Ctrl+O = save file
# Ctrl+X = exit
# Ctrl+W = search
# Bottom bar shows all shortcuts in plain English

# VIM - powerful but needs learning
vim /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
# Press i = enter INSERT mode (now you can type)
# Press Esc = back to command mode
# Type :wq = save and quit
# Type :q! = quit WITHOUT saving (emergency exit!)
# Type /word = search for word

# QUICK EDITS without opening editor
sed -i 's/old/new/g' config.txt     # replace 'old' with 'new' in file
echo "new line" >> config.txt       # append a line to end of file
```

---

## Mind Map - The Big Picture

```
LINUX ESSENTIALS
|
+-- FILESYSTEM
|   +-- / is root of everything
|   +-- /etc = configs
|   +-- /var/log = log files
|   +-- /home/user = your space
|   +-- /tmp = temp (wiped on reboot)
|   +-- /proc = virtual (live process info)
|
+-- NAVIGATION
|   +-- pwd (where am I?)
|   +-- ls (what is here?)
|   +-- cd (move around)
|   +-- mkdir, touch, cp, mv, rm
|
+-- READING FILES
|   +-- cat (dump to screen)
|   +-- less (scroll through)
|   +-- tail -f (live follow)
|   +-- grep (search inside)
|
+-- PERMISSIONS
|   +-- rwx = read/write/execute
|   +-- 3 groups: owner, group, others
|   +-- chmod (change permissions)
|   +-- chown (change owner)
|   +-- Key recipes: 600=private, 755=script
|
+-- PROCESSES
|   +-- ps aux (see all)
|   +-- top/htop (live monitor)
|   +-- kill PID (SIGTERM = graceful)
|   +-- kill -9 PID (SIGKILL = force)
|   +-- & and nohup (background)
|
+-- PIPES & REDIRECTION
|   +-- | (pipe: chain commands)
|   +-- > (overwrite to file)
|   +-- >> (append to file)
|   +-- 2>&1 (merge errors into output)
|
+-- SSH
|   +-- ssh user@host (connect)
|   +-- ssh-keygen (generate key pair)
|   +-- chmod 600 private key!
|   +-- scp (copy files over SSH)
|
+-- PACKAGES
    +-- apt update (refresh list)
    +-- apt install X (install)
    +-- sudo prefix for admin commands
```

---

## How This Connects to Other Topics

- **Git**: You run `git` commands from bash. Every `git push` is a Linux command.
- **Docker**: Containers ARE Linux. `docker exec -it container bash` drops you into a Linux shell.
- **CI/CD Pipelines**: Pipeline steps are bash scripts on Linux runners.
- **Kubernetes**: `kubectl exec -it pod -- bash` gives you a Linux shell inside a pod.
- **AWS/Cloud**: EC2 instances are Linux VMs. You SSH in, manage processes, tail logs.

---

## Common Beginner Mistakes

1. **Forgetting sudo** — `Permission denied`? Add `sudo` in front.
2. **rm -rf wrong path** — Always `ls` first to confirm what is there before deleting.
3. **File not writable** — `ls -la` to check permissions before editing.
4. **SSH Permission denied** — Check: `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa`, correct username, key in authorized_keys.
5. **Command not found** — Program installed but not in PATH. Use `which programname` to find it.
6. **Lost in directories** — Run `pwd` to see where you are. `cd ~` always gets you home.
7. **grep finds nothing** — Try `grep -i` for case-insensitive. The case might not match.

---

## References and Further Learning

### Videos (Watch These!)
- **Linux Command Line Full Course** by tutorialinux: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZtqBQ68cfJc
  - 5-hour beginner to intermediate. Go at your own pace.
- **60 Linux Commands you NEED to know** by NetworkChuck: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gd7BXuUQ91w
  - Fast-paced, practical, fun. Great overview.

### Free Books and Articles
- **The Linux Command Line** (free full book): https://linuxcommand.org/tlcl.php
  - Best free beginner book by William Shotts. Read chapters 1-10 first.
- **Linux Journey** (interactive exercises): https://linuxjourney.com/
  - Gamified step-by-step lessons. Do 15 min per day.
- **Explain Shell** (paste any command, get it explained): https://explainshell.com/
  - Paste `find /var -name '*.log' -mtime -1` and see every part explained visually.

### Diagrams and Cheatsheets
- **Linux Filesystem Hierarchy Diagram**: search 'Linux FHS diagram' on Google Images
- **chmod calculator** (visual permissions tool): https://chmod-calculator.com/
- **Bash reference manual**: https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/bash.html

### Practice (hands-on is the only way)
- **OverTheWire Bandit** (gamified Linux challenges from zero): https://overthewire.org/wargames/bandit/
  - Level 0 starts with just `ssh`. By level 30 you know Linux well.
- **TryHackMe Linux Fundamentals** (browser terminal, no install needed): https://tryhackme.com/module/linux-fundamentals

questions.prompt: Which directory contains system-wide configuration files on Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc holds configuration files like nginx.conf, sshd_config, and hosts. /bin holds essential binaries, /var holds variable data like logs, /usr holds user-installed programs.

questions.prompt: What does `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa` do?
choices: Gives the owner read+write, removes all permissions from group and others
Makes the file executable for the owner
Removes all permissions from the file
questions.explanation: 600 = rw------- : owner gets read(4)+write(2)=6, group gets 0, others get 0. SSH requires this exact permission on private keys.

questions.prompt: Which signal does `kill 1234` send by default?
questions.explanation: By default kill sends SIGTERM (15), asking the process to exit gracefully. SIGKILL (kill -9) forces immediate termination and cannot be caught.

questions.prompt: What does this command print?
questions.explanation: tr translates characters. 'a-z' → 'A-Z' uppercases every lowercase letter, producing HELLO WORLD.

questions.prompt: To recursively change the owner of /var/www/html to user www-data group www-data, you use: `sudo chown ___ www-data:www-data /var/www/html`
questions.explanation: chown -R (recursive) traverses subdirectories. Without -R only the top-level directory ownership changes.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid ways to run a command in the background? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: & runs immediately in background, nohup detaches from terminal (survives logout), Ctrl+Z suspends then bg resumes in background. sudo just elevates privileges.

questions.prompt: Order these steps to find which process is consuming the most CPU:
items: Press P to sort by CPU
Identify the PID of the top process
questions.explanation: First SSH in, then run top, sort by CPU with P, then identify the PID. top shows live stats and P sorts by CPU usage.

questions.prompt: Match each directory to its primary purpose:
questions.explanation: /etc=configs, /var/log=logs, /tmp=temp files, /proc=process info as virtual files.

questions.prompt: What does `2>&1` mean in `java -jar app.jar > app.log 2>&1`?
choices: Redirect stderr (fd 2) to the same destination as stdout (fd 1)
questions.explanation: 2>&1 means 'redirect file descriptor 2 (stderr) to wherever fd 1 (stdout) currently points'. Since stdout is redirected to app.log, both streams go to app.log.

questions.prompt: Which command finds all .log files modified in the last 24 hours under /var/log?
questions.explanation: find with -name for glob matching and -mtime -1 for 'less than 1 day ago'. grep searches file contents, ls can't filter by modification time easily, and locate has no -mtime flag.

questions.prompt: What permission level is required on an SSH private key file for SSH to use it?
questions.explanation: SSH requires private key files to be 600 (rw-------). If group or others have any permission, SSH refuses with 'Permissions too open' error.

questions.prompt: To append text to a file (not overwrite it), you use the redirection operator ___
questions.explanation: >> appends. > overwrites. This is critical — using > when you mean >> destroys existing log data.

questions.prompt: What is the output of this command sequence?
questions.explanation: mkdir creates 'test', cd enters it, pwd prints the full absolute path which includes 'test' appended to the current directory.

questions.prompt: What does `ps aux | grep nginx | grep -v grep` do?
choices: Lists all processes then removes nginx entries
Shows nginx processes excluding the grep process itself
questions.explanation: ps aux lists all processes. grep nginx filters for nginx. grep -v grep removes the 'grep nginx' process from results (which would also match 'nginx').

questions.prompt: Which of the following directories are typically found under Linux root (/)? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: /bin, /etc, /var are standard Linux FHS directories. /windows and /library are macOS/Windows concepts not typically found on Linux.

questions.prompt: Which command generates a new SSH key pair using the ed25519 algorithm?
questions.explanation: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 creates a modern ed25519 key pair. ed25519 is preferred over RSA for new keys — smaller, faster, equally secure.

questions.prompt: Order these steps to securely copy a file from local machine to a remote server:
items: Verify the file exists on remote with ssh+ls
Ensure SSH key is set up
Check destination path exists on remote
questions.explanation: First ensure SSH works, verify the destination exists, copy with scp, then verify the copy succeeded.

questions.prompt: What does `chmod +x script.sh` do?
choices: Makes script.sh executable for the owner only
Adds execute permission for all (owner, group, others)
questions.explanation: +x without specifying a user class adds execute for all. Use a (all) implicitly. To restrict: chmod u+x adds only for owner.

questions.prompt: To search recursively for the string 'NullPointerException' in all .log files under /var/log, you would run: `grep ___ 'NullPointerException' /var/log/*.log`
questions.explanation: grep -r (recursive) traverses subdirectories. Without -R, grep only searches the directly specified files.

questions.prompt: Your app needs write access to /var/log/myapp. The app runs as user 'appuser'. What is the correct fix?
questions.explanation: Changing ownership to appuser is the secure, correct approach. chmod 777 is dangerous (anyone can write). Running as root is a security risk. chmod +r only adds read, not write.

flashcards.front: What directory contains all system configuration files?
What does chmod 600 mean in octal notation?
flashcards.back: rw------- : owner read(4)+write(2)=6, group 0, others 0. Required for SSH private keys.
SIGTERM (15) asks process to exit gracefully (can be caught/ignored). SIGKILL (9) immediately terminates — cannot be caught. Use SIGTERM first, SIGKILL as last resort.
Connects stdout of left command to stdin of right command. e.g. cat access.log | grep ERROR — feeds log content to grep.

flashcards.front: How do you run a command that survives terminal logout?
flashcards.back: nohup ./app & — nohup ignores SIGHUP (terminal close), & backgrounds the process.
Redirect file descriptor 2 (stderr) to wherever fd 1 (stdout) points. cmd > file.log 2>&1 sends both stdout and stderr to file.log.

flashcards.front: What command finds files modified in the last 24 hours?
What is the difference between > and >> redirection?
flashcards.back: > overwrites the file (destroys existing content). >> appends to the file. Always use >> for logs.

project.brief:
## Project: Linux Server Hardening Checklist

You've just been handed SSH access to a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 VPS that will run your Spring Boot API in production. Before deploying your app, you need to harden the server.

**Your task:** Design a minimal but effective server hardening plan. For each action, briefly explain *why* it matters (1–2 sentences). No need to run commands — think through the decisions.

Consider: default credentials, SSH configuration, user privileges, firewall rules, log monitoring, software updates, and file permissions for your app.

This is a brain exercise. Write your plan as bullet points or a numbered list.

checklist.text: Disabled root SSH login (PermitRootLogin no in sshd_config) and justified why
Created a non-root user for the app with minimum required permissions
Configured SSH key-based auth only (PasswordAuthentication no) and noted the risk if done wrong
Identified which ports to open with a firewall (e.g. ufw) and why others should be closed
Described where app logs go, who owns those files, and what permissions they need
Mentioned automatic security updates or a patching strategy
Identified at least one common mistake (e.g. chmod 777, running as root) and how to avoid it

hints: Think about the principle of least privilege: every user and process should have only the access it needs, nothing more.
SSH hardening has two layers: authentication method (keys > passwords) and authorization (which user can log in). Consider both.
A firewall should follow a 'deny all, allow specific' strategy. Ask yourself: what ports does my app actually need? (hint: 22 for SSH, 8080 or 443 for app, nothing else)
